title: Implementing an OS Service
text: Implementing an OS Service Uniface enables you use an operating system service to send commands and return output from an operating system. To create and use an OS service: Create a signature for an operating system service with the Implementation Type set to Operating System Command . Two default, non-editable operations are available, COMMAND and COMMANDOUT . In the calling component, call the OS service using the activate statement. For example, if you created a component called OSUNIX: activate "OSUNIX".COMMAND("at -fm myscript now") activate "OSUNIX".COMMANDOUT("ls -l mydir", $LISTING$) Copy Output is passed to the $LISTING$ variable. In your application’s assignment file, specify a path that directs the operating system component to the operating system server. For example: [PATHS] $SERVER1:=TCP:PHOENIX|leda|?|CSV [SERVICES_EXEC] OSUNIX=$SERVER1:OSUNIX Copy Related Links Define a Component Signature Use the Signature Editor to create or modify component signatures. Signatures of Uniface components are automatically generated during compilation. Signatures of non-Uniface components can be created or imported. [PATHS] Use the [PATHS] section of the assignment file to define paths to network and database connectors, which connect to a Uniface Server, DBMS, or to another path. Path assignments can include path-to-connector assignments and path-to-path assignments. [SERVICES_EXEC] Use the [SERVICES_EXEC] section of the assignment file to direct the execution of service components to a network path, COM, or SOAP connector, and to redefine the behavior of COM and SOAP connectors for specific components. Operating System Commands Uniface enables you to pass commands to the operating system and catch the returned output using the COMMAND and COMMANDOUT operations. Was this topic helpful? Like Dislike
